$comment: Kazakhstan evidence ledger — Phase 1 (country baseline) + Phase 2 (official exchange terms). Objects validate against schemas/geo/evidence.schema.json. exchange=null means country-level. Sprint 8B items (checked_at 2026-07-03) are re-used only where inside the config/geo/freshness.json windows; live captures for them are in reports/evidence/geo/kazakhstan/2026-07-03/ (untracked). No live proxied test has run yet — capture is null everywhere (Phase 3 blocked on PROXY_KZ).

evidence.claim: Kazakhstan regulates digital assets under Law No. 193-VII 'On Digital Assets' (2023-02-06, effective 2023-04-01), amended 2026-01-16 to add digital financial assets incl. stablecoins.
evidence.source_title: National Bank of Kazakhstan — Regulation of the digital assets market
evidence.extract: Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan 'On Digital Assets in the Republic of Kazakhstan' № 193-VII LRK (February 6, 2023) … amendments … dated January 16, 2026, introducing digital financial assets (DFAs) including stablecoins and tokenized assets.
evidence.interpretation: Crypto is legal and regulated in Kazakhstan; a formal licensing regime exists.
evidence.limitations: Page content summarised via fetch; full legal text not captured.

evidence.claim: A comprehensive digital-asset framework took force 2026-05-01: NBK licenses unsecured digital asset service providers outside the AIFC; AFSA supervises inside the AIFC; ARDFM oversees DFAs except stablecoins.
evidence.source_title: NBK — Kazakhstan introduces regulation of digital asset turnover
evidence.extract: Starting from May 1, 2026, licensing of unsecured digital asset exchange operators as well as the registration of operators of digital asset trading platforms and digital financial asset platforms is carried out through submission of documentation to the National Bank.
evidence.interpretation: Since 2026-05-01 exchanges serving Kazakhstan need an NBK license/registration OR an AFSA (AIFC) authorisation. This postdates Sprint 8B (2026-07-03 research covered the pre-existing AIFC-only regime).
evidence.limitations: Announcement does not spell out cross-border enforcement mechanics for foreign platforms.

evidence.claim: AFSA publicly warned that OKX, HTX, Bitget and MEXC operate as unlicensed digital-asset platforms toward Kazakhstan citizens; Article 11(5) of the Digital Assets Law prohibits organising circulation of digital assets without a licence.
evidence.source_title: AFSA Warning on Unlicensed Digital Asset Platforms
evidence.extract: The organisation of circulation of digital assets within the territory of the Republic of Kazakhstan is prohibited without an appropriate licence or authorisation issued under the applicable law of the AIFC or the legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan.
evidence.interpretation: Direct Tier-A regulator warning naming three of the six CBW exchanges (okx, bitget, mexc). Gate 02 (regulatory_safety) trigger 'regulator_warning' applies to those three for Kazakhstan.
evidence.limitations: The AFSA page itself shows no publication date; 2026-04-29 comes from secondary reporting (ev-kz-enforcement-006). The warning frames itself as a consumer risk warning while citing the statutory prohibition.

evidence.claim: Presidential decree of 2026-07-07 grants income-tax exemption for digital-asset trades on licensed platforms and supports stablecoin settlement and tokenized instruments.
evidence.source_title: Kazakhstan president signs decree to boost crypto adoption with tax breaks and stablecoin payments
evidence.extract: If you trade crypto on a licensed, government-approved platform in Kazakhstan, the profits won't be taxed as personal income.
evidence.interpretation: Pro-adoption policy signal, but the benefit is explicitly tied to LICENSED platforms — reinforces the licensed/unlicensed split.
evidence.limitations: Secondary source only; official decree text not captured. Tier C — context, not a publishable critical claim.

evidence.claim: Kazakhstan authorities blocked over 1,100 unlicensed crypto exchange platforms in the year to January 2026.
evidence.source_title: Kazakhstan blocks 1,100 unlicensed crypto exchanges in crackdown
evidence.extract: Officials say the goal is to curb money laundering and consumer risk while building a regulated digital asset hub.
evidence.interpretation: Active technical enforcement (blocking) against unlicensed platforms — access from Kazakhstan is not guaranteed even where an exchange's own terms allow it.
evidence.limitations: Secondary source; no official list of blocked platforms captured; does not name the six CBW exchanges.

evidence.claim: Kazakhstan's Financial Monitoring Agency (AFM) closed ~two dozen illegal exchanges in the year to April 2026 and froze 9.7M USDT (RAKS Exchange case); AFSA's 2026-04-29 statement stressed that even global-leader platforms must be authorised locally.
evidence.source_title: Kazakhstan targets major cryptocurrency exchanges over illegal activities (2026-04-29)
evidence.extract: Even if a platform is considered a global leader, it must be authorized to operate within the jurisdiction.
evidence.interpretation: Corroborates and dates the AFSA warning (ev-kz-afsa-warning-003); documents parallel AML enforcement.
evidence.limitations: Secondary source; used mainly to date the Tier-A warning.

evidence.claim: The dominant retail banking/payment rails in Kazakhstan are Kaspi and Halyk Bank (plus ForteBank, Jusan, Bank CenterCredit, Freedom Bank); crypto P2P in KZT commonly settles via transfers to these banks.
evidence.source_title: Integrating Kazakhstan payment systems: Kaspi, Halyk, Epay (+ corroborated by P2P method lists on major venues)
evidence.interpretation: Payment-environment context for user-fit research: Kaspi/Halyk presence in an exchange's P2P method list is the practical KZT on-ramp signal.
evidence.limitations: Tier C aggregate context; per-exchange KZT P2P support must be verified per exchange (Phase 3/kz-t07/kz-t09), not inferred from this.

evidence.claim: Bybit Limited holds AFSA license AFSA-A-LA-2024-0027, status Active, effective 2024-09-25, covering money services, custody, dealing/managing investments and operating a digital asset trading facility.
evidence.source_title: AFSA Public Register — Bybit Limited licence details
evidence.extract: -Providing Money Services -Providing Custody -Dealing in Investments as Principal -Dealing in Investments as Agent -Managing Investments -Operating a Digital Asset Trading Facility
evidence.interpretation: Bybit is the only one of the six CBW exchanges with a verified local (AIFC/AFSA) authorisation. Re-verified fresh this run (first verified in Sprint 8B, 2026-07-03).
evidence.limitations: License covers the AIFC-regulated Bybit Kazakhstan product; relationship between that entity and global bybit.com accounts/bonuses is not established (see unknowns).

evidence.claim: Bybit Kazakhstan launched the country's first fully regulated P2P platform (Nov 2025) under AFSA license AFSA-A-LA-2024-0027: KZT settlement via verified Halyk Bank cards through corporate accounts of AFSA-licensed institutions; limits 2.5M KZT/transaction, 5M KZT/day; no P2P commission.
evidence.source_title: Bybit Kazakhstan Launches the Country's First Fully Regulated P2P Platform (Chainwire press release, 2025-11-26)
evidence.extract: The system preserves the familiar structure of P2P trading while adding controls intended to enhance reliability and improve clarity in fiat transfers.
evidence.interpretation: KZT P2P exists on the licensed local Bybit product, initially Halyk-only. Tier B (corporate press release republished by wire) — supports verified_with_limits, not full verification of current state.
evidence.limitations: Initial-rollout description from Nov 2025; current bank coverage, limits and liquidity unverified (kz-t07 P2P snapshots pending).

evidence.claim: Kazakhstan is not on Bybit's global 14-jurisdiction exclusion list (global Terms of Service).
evidence.source_title: Bybit Terms of Service — restricted jurisdictions (dated capture on file, Sprint 8B)
evidence.interpretation: Bybit's own global terms do not exclude Kazakhstan.
evidence.limitations: Sprint 8B capture (reports/evidence/geo/kazakhstan/2026-07-03/): bybit.com blocks automated fetch, so this could not be re-fetched this run. 11 days old — inside the 30-day restricted_country_terms freshness window.

evidence.claim: Kazakhstan is absent from MEXC's prohibited-jurisdictions list in its User Agreement (document last updated 2025-05-29).
evidence.extract: North Korea, Cuba, Sudan, Iran, Mainland China, Singapore, United States, United Kingdom, Hong Kong, Russian-controlled regions of Ukraine, Canada [Kazakhstan not listed]
evidence.interpretation: MEXC's own terms do not restrict Kazakhstan users. Conflicts with the AFSA regulator warning (ev-kz-afsa-warning-003) — see cf-kz-mexc-terms-vs-regulator.
evidence.limitations: List is explicitly 'non-exclusive and subject to change'. Own-terms absence says nothing about local legality.

evidence.claim: Kazakhstan is absent from OKX's Risk & Compliance Disclosure restricted-locations list (document last updated 2026-07-08).
evidence.source_title: OKX Risk & Compliance Disclosure — restricted locations
evidence.extract: Afghanistan, Canada, Cuba, Hong Kong, Iran, India, Japan, Malaysia, Nepal, North Korea, Syria, the Crimea, Donetsk and Luhansk regions of Ukraine, certain jurisdictions within the United States of America … and Uzbekistan. [Kazakhstan not listed]
evidence.interpretation: OKX's own current disclosure (updated a week before this check) does not restrict Kazakhstan. Conflicts with the AFSA regulator warning — see cf-kz-okx-terms-vs-regulator.
evidence.limitations: Own-terms absence says nothing about local licensing status.

evidence.claim: Kazakhstan is explicitly named in Bitget's Terms of Use Prohibited Countries list (document last updated 2026-06-16).
evidence.source_title: Bitget Terms of Use — Prohibited Countries
evidence.extract: Austria, Canada, Crimea, Donetsk, Luhansk, Cuba, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Iran, Kazakhstan, North Korea, Singapore, Sudan, the United States (…), Iraq, Libya, Yemen, Afghanistan, …
evidence.interpretation: Re-confirms the Sprint 8B finding on the CURRENT terms version: Bitget itself prohibits Kazakhstan. Consistent with AFSA naming Bitget as unlicensed.
evidence.limitations: None material — own terms and regulator agree.

evidence.claim: Kazakhstan is absent from KuCoin's Terms of Use Restricted Locations (Article 17(5); document last updated 2026-01-29).
evidence.source_title: KuCoin Terms of Use — Article 17(5) Restricted Locations
evidence.extract: United States (…), Singapore, the mainland of China and Hong Kong, Malaysia, Ontario, and British Columbia of Canada, France, Netherlands, the Crimea, Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia and Kherson regions of Ukraine [Kazakhstan not listed]
evidence.interpretation: KuCoin's own terms do not restrict Kazakhstan; KuCoin was NOT named in the AFSA warning. Still no positive confirmation of KZ availability or local authorisation.
evidence.limitations: List updatable at platform's discretion; no KZ licence found on AFSA register.

evidence.claim: Kazakhstan is absent from BingX's Disclaimer restricted-jurisdictions list (document dated 2023-12-11, current live version).
evidence.extract: Afghanistan, Burundi, Cambodia, Canada, Central African Republic, China (Mainland), Cuba, DR Congo, Hong Kong SAR, Iran, Laos, Macau SAR, Myanmar, Netherlands, North Korea, Panama, Singapore, Somalia, United Kingdom, United States (…), Crimea, Donetsk, Luhansk [Kazakhstan not listed]
evidence.interpretation: BingX's own disclaimer does not restrict Kazakhstan; BingX was NOT named in the AFSA warning. No positive confirmation of KZ availability or local authorisation.
evidence.limitations: Document is dated 2023-12-11 and predates Kazakhstan's 2026 framework entirely; it is the current published version but its age lowers its weight for present-day eligibility.
